rules.title: Administrator-style wildcard permission
rules.summary: Detects an Allow statement that grants every action against every resource.
rules.confidence_basis: The analyzer observes an explicit Allow with both Action and Resource set to a full wildcard.

mappings.title: IAM policies should not allow full administrative privileges
mappings.rationale: Both checks identify customer-managed IAM policy statements that allow all actions on all resources.

rules.title: Wildcard action allowed
rules.summary: Detects wildcard patterns in allowed IAM actions, including service-wide and partial wildcards.
rules.confidence_basis: The action pattern is explicit policy evidence; severity rises for full or service-wide wildcards.

mappings.title: IAM customer managed policies that you create should not allow wildcard actions for services
mappings.rationale: The lab also flags partial wildcard action patterns, so its condition is broader than the Security Hub control.

rules.title: Wildcard resource scope
rules.summary: Detects allowed actions that apply to every resource.
rules.confidence_basis: The Resource wildcard is explicit, although some AWS actions do not support resource-level scoping.

mappings.title: IAM policies should not allow full administrative privileges
mappings.rationale: Wildcard resource scope contributes to administrative breadth, but this rule can trigger without Action being a full wildcard.

rules.title: Broad S3 write permission
rules.summary: Detects write-capable S3 actions granted across wildcard bucket or object resources.
rules.confidence_basis: The policy grants broad S3 write capability, but effective access can still be reduced by boundaries, resource policies, or explicit denies.

mappings.title: Data Destruction
mappings.rationale: Broad S3 write and delete capability can enable destructive impact, but the rule identifies permission rather than observed destruction.

rules.title: Sensitive action without MFA condition
rules.summary: Detects privileged user or group permissions that do not require an MFA-present condition.
rules.confidence_basis: The statement lacks an MFA condition, but other identity controls or session policies may still require MFA.

mappings.title: MFA should be enabled for all IAM users that have a console password
mappings.rationale: Both concern MFA protection for IAM users, while this rule evaluates policy conditions rather than credential-report enrollment.

rules.title: User MFA is disabled
rules.summary: Detects an IAM user with console-password access and no registered MFA device.
rules.confidence_basis: Credential-report evidence directly states password and MFA status when native AWS input is used.

mappings.title: MFA should be enabled for all IAM users that have a console password
mappings.rationale: The rule and control evaluate the same console-password and MFA enrollment condition.

mappings.title: Ensure MFA is enabled for all IAM users that have a console password
mappings.rationale: The analyzer directly evaluates the credential-report fields used by this benchmark recommendation.

rules.title: Long-lived access key
rules.summary: Detects an active IAM access key older than the analyzer rotation threshold.
rules.confidence_basis: The active status and key age are derived directly from credential-report timestamps.

mappings.title: IAM users' access keys should be rotated every 90 days or less
mappings.rationale: The lab uses the same 90-day rotation threshold as this Security Hub control.

mappings.title: Ensure access keys are rotated every 90 days or less
mappings.rationale: The analyzer and benchmark recommendation use the same 90-day access-key rotation threshold.

rules.title: Broad or external role trust
rules.summary: Detects role trust policies that allow public or cross-account principals, with severity adjusted for recognized guardrails.
rules.confidence_basis: The external principal is explicit, but effective assumability also depends on caller permissions, conditions, organization boundaries, and external IDs.

mappings.title: Trusted Relationship
mappings.rationale: Cross-account role trust can expose a trusted access path, but the rule does not establish that an adversary has compromised the trusted party.

rules.title: Broad allow uses NotAction
rules.summary: Detects Allow statements that grant the complement of an excluded action list.
rules.confidence_basis: NotAction is explicit policy syntax; severity rises when the statement also applies to every resource.

mappings.title: IAM policies should not allow full administrative privileges
mappings.rationale: An Allow with NotAction can approach administrative breadth, but it is not necessarily equivalent to allowing every action.

rules.title: Broad allow uses NotResource
rules.summary: Detects Allow statements that apply to every applicable resource except an exclusion list.
rules.confidence_basis: NotResource is explicit policy syntax; severity rises for sensitive or wildcard action sets.

mappings.title: IAM policies should not allow full administrative privileges
mappings.rationale: A broad NotResource allow can undermine least privilege, but it does not always provide full administrative access.

rules.title: Stale active access key
rules.summary: Detects an active access key that has not been used within the analyzer inactivity threshold.
rules.confidence_basis: Key status and last-used age come directly from credential-report evidence.

mappings.title: Unused IAM user credentials should be removed
mappings.rationale: Both detect unused active credentials, but the lab uses a 90-day threshold while the Security Hub parameter can differ.

mappings.title: Ensure credentials unused for 45 days or greater are disabled
mappings.rationale: The benchmark uses 45 days, while this analyzer intentionally uses a 90-day inactivity threshold.

rules.title: Stale console password
rules.summary: Detects an active IAM console password that has not been used within the analyzer inactivity threshold.
rules.confidence_basis: Password status and last-used age come directly from credential-report evidence.

mappings.title: Unused IAM user credentials should be removed
mappings.rationale: Both detect unused IAM credentials, but evaluation thresholds and evidence collection can differ.

mappings.title: Ensure credentials unused for 45 days or greater are disabled
mappings.rationale: The benchmark uses 45 days, while this analyzer intentionally uses a 90-day inactivity threshold.

rules.title: Root account has an active access key
rules.summary: Detects an active programmatic access key for the AWS account root user.
rules.confidence_basis: Credential-report evidence directly identifies active root access-key slots.

mappings.title: IAM root user access key should not exist
mappings.rationale: The rule and control evaluate the same root access-key condition.

mappings.title: Ensure no access keys are associated with the root user account
mappings.rationale: The analyzer directly detects active root access keys from the credential report.

rules.title: Root account MFA is disabled
rules.summary: Detects an AWS root user with an active console password and no MFA device.
rules.confidence_basis: Credential-report evidence directly states root password and MFA status.

mappings.title: MFA should be enabled for the root user
mappings.rationale: The rule and control evaluate the same root MFA condition without claiming hardware-MFA coverage.

mappings.title: Ensure MFA is enabled for the root user account
mappings.rationale: The analyzer directly evaluates root MFA enrollment from credential-report evidence.

rules.title: Permissions boundary does not constrain access
rules.summary: Detects a permissions boundary that itself allows every action against every resource.
rules.confidence_basis: The boundary document explicitly allows full wildcard access, so it does not lower the maximum permission set.

mappings.title: IAM policies should not allow full administrative privileges
mappings.rationale: The wildcard syntax matches administrative breadth, but a permissions boundary limits rather than directly grants effective permissions.

rules.title: S3 public access block is incomplete
rules.summary: Detects an S3 bucket with one or more Block Public Access controls disabled or missing.
rules.confidence_basis: The rule directly evaluates all four bucket-level Block Public Access booleans.

mappings.title: S3 general purpose buckets should have block public access settings enabled
mappings.rationale: Both evaluate S3 Block Public Access, but Security Hub can incorporate account-level settings that this bucket evidence does not model.

mappings.title: S3 general purpose buckets should block public access
mappings.rationale: The rule checks whether the bucket-level public-access protections required by this control are all enabled.

mappings.title: Ensure that S3 Buckets are configured with Block public access
mappings.rationale: The analyzer directly evaluates the bucket-level configuration named by this benchmark recommendation.

rules.title: Bucket ACL grants public access
rules.summary: Detects an effective public or broadly authenticated S3 ACL grant.
rules.confidence_basis: The ACL grantee is explicitly public and the analyzer confirms that ACLs are neither ignored nor disabled by Object Ownership.

mappings.title: S3 general purpose buckets should block public read access
mappings.rationale: A public ACL can grant read access, but this rule also detects other public ACL permissions.

mappings.title: S3 general purpose buckets should block public write access
mappings.rationale: A public ACL can grant write access, but this rule also detects public read or metadata permissions.

mappings.title: Ensure that S3 Buckets are configured with Block public access
mappings.rationale: Public ACL exposure is a condition the benchmark's Block Public Access recommendation is intended to prevent.

rules.title: Bucket policy allows an effectively public principal
rules.summary: Detects an S3 bucket-policy Allow whose broad principal lacks an AWS-recognized fixed-value guardrail.
rules.confidence_basis: The policy is public under modeled S3 policy rules, but account-level Block Public Access and unmodeled policy context can affect runtime exposure.

mappings.title: S3 general purpose buckets should block public read access
mappings.rationale: A public bucket policy can grant read access, while this rule also covers non-read actions.

mappings.title: S3 general purpose buckets should block public write access
mappings.rationale: A public bucket policy can grant write access, while this rule also covers non-write actions.

mappings.title: S3 general purpose buckets should block public access
mappings.rationale: The rule identifies the effective public policy exposure that S3 Block Public Access is designed to prevent.

mappings.title: Ensure that S3 Buckets are configured with Block public access
mappings.rationale: The detected public policy is directly addressed by enabling and enforcing Block Public Access.

rules.title: Bucket lacks an explicit encryption configuration
rules.summary: Detects the absence of an explicit bucket-level default encryption configuration.
rules.confidence_basis: The configuration absence is explicit in the evidence, while the low severity acknowledges S3 baseline SSE-S3 encryption.

mappings.title: S3 general purpose buckets should be encrypted at rest with AWS KMS keys
mappings.rationale: The rule identifies missing explicit encryption configuration but does not require KMS or claim that objects are stored unencrypted.

rules.title: Bucket versioning is not enabled
rules.summary: Detects an S3 bucket whose versioning status is disabled or suspended.
rules.confidence_basis: The bucket versioning state is directly represented in the normalized evidence.

mappings.title: S3 general purpose buckets should have versioning enabled
mappings.rationale: The rule and control evaluate the same bucket versioning condition.

rules.title: Bucket access control lists remain enabled
rules.summary: Detects an S3 Object Ownership mode that leaves ACL-based access control active.
rules.confidence_basis: The Object Ownership setting directly determines whether ACLs can affect authorization.

mappings.title: ACLs should not be used to manage user access to S3 general purpose buckets
mappings.rationale: The rule detects the ACL-enabled ownership modes that this control recommends replacing with BucketOwnerEnforced.

rules.title: Sensitive service port permits public traffic
rules.summary: Detects public IPv4 or IPv6 ingress to cataloged administrative, database, or control-plane service ports.
rules.confidence_basis: The security-group source and port are explicit, but end-to-end exposure depends on optional reachability context and attached resources.

mappings.title: Security groups should only allow unrestricted incoming traffic for authorized ports
mappings.rationale: Both flag unrestricted ingress, while this rule targets a curated sensitive-service catalog and supports broad public CIDRs.

mappings.title: Security groups should not allow unrestricted access to ports with high risk
mappings.rationale: The rule covers high-risk ports and additional service categories, with context-sensitive severity.

mappings.title: Security groups should not allow ingress from 0.0.0.0/0 to remote server administration ports
mappings.rationale: The rule includes public IPv4 access to SSH and RDP, but also evaluates other sensitive services.

mappings.title: Security groups should not allow ingress from ::/0 to remote server administration ports
mappings.rationale: The rule includes public IPv6 access to SSH and RDP, but also evaluates other sensitive services.

mappings.title: Ensure no security groups allow ingress from 0.0.0.0/0 to remote server administration ports
mappings.rationale: The analyzer covers the benchmark's IPv4 SSH and RDP cases plus additional public CIDRs and services.

mappings.title: Ensure no security groups allow ingress from ::/0 to remote server administration ports
mappings.rationale: The analyzer covers the benchmark's IPv6 SSH and RDP cases plus additional public networks and services.

rules.title: All inbound ports permit public traffic
rules.summary: Detects a security-group ingress rule that allows every port from a public IPv4 or IPv6 network.
rules.confidence_basis: The all-port public rule is explicit, but actual internet reachability depends on routing, attached resources, and optional path evidence.

mappings.title: Security groups should only allow unrestricted incoming traffic for authorized ports
mappings.rationale: An all-port public rule is a direct unrestricted-ingress condition.

mappings.title: Security groups should not allow unrestricted access to ports with high risk
mappings.rationale: Allowing all ports necessarily includes every high-risk port evaluated by the control.

rules.title: Security group permits unrestricted outbound traffic
rules.summary: Detects all-protocol, all-port egress from a security group to a public IPv4 or IPv6 network.
rules.confidence_basis: The egress permission is explicit, but an exploitable exfiltration path depends on workload compromise and end-to-end network routing.

mappings.title: Exfiltration Over C2 Channel
mappings.rationale: Unrestricted egress can enable exfiltration or command-and-control traffic, but the rule does not observe data transfer.

rules.title: Root account console login
rules.summary: Detects a successful AWS ConsoleLogin event performed by the root identity.
rules.confidence_basis: CloudTrail explicitly records the successful root identity type and ConsoleLogin event.

mappings.title: Valid Accounts: Cloud Accounts
mappings.rationale: Root console use can represent use of a valid cloud account, but the event alone does not prove credential compromise.

rules.title: MFA device was disabled or deleted
rules.summary: Detects a successful API event that deactivates or deletes an IAM MFA device.
rules.confidence_basis: The successful CloudTrail event directly records an MFA defense being removed.

mappings.title: Account Manipulation
mappings.rationale: Removing MFA changes an account security property in a way that can support persistence or continued access.

rules.title: Security group configuration changed
rules.summary: Detects successful CloudTrail events that add, remove, or modify security-group rules.
rules.confidence_basis: The successful event directly records a cloud network-control modification, though the resulting exposure requires separate analysis.

mappings.title: Modify Cloud Compute Configurations
mappings.rationale: Changing security-group rules is a direct modification of cloud compute networking configuration.

rules.title: Bucket access policy changed
rules.summary: Detects successful changes to S3 bucket policy or public-access settings.
rules.confidence_basis: The successful CloudTrail event directly records an S3 access-control change, while the resulting policy effect requires configuration analysis.

mappings.title: Data Manipulation
mappings.rationale: Changing bucket access policy can enable later data manipulation or exposure, but the event does not itself show data modification.

rules.title: IAM policy configuration changed
rules.summary: Detects successful IAM policy creation, attachment, version, or inline-policy changes.
rules.confidence_basis: The successful event directly records a permission-policy change, while its privilege impact requires policy-diff analysis.

mappings.title: Account Manipulation
mappings.rationale: IAM policy changes can alter account or role permissions to support persistence or privilege escalation.

rules.title: Repeated API failures from one actor and source
rules.summary: Detects a threshold burst of failed AWS API calls grouped by actor and source IP within a sliding time window.
rules.confidence_basis: The failure burst is measured directly, but benign automation errors and permission drift can produce the same pattern.

mappings.title: Brute Force
mappings.rationale: Repeated failures can indicate credential guessing or probing, but the detector also includes non-authentication API errors.

rules.title: IAM user console login did not use MFA
rules.summary: Detects a successful IAM-user ConsoleLogin event whose additional event data records MFA as unused.
rules.confidence_basis: CloudTrail directly records both the successful IAM-user login and MFAUsed value.

mappings.title: Valid Accounts: Cloud Accounts
mappings.rationale: A password-only console login can reflect use of a valid cloud account, but the event alone does not establish adversary control.

rules.title: Persistent cloud credential was created
rules.summary: Detects successful creation of IAM access keys, login profiles, signing certificates, or service-specific credentials.
rules.confidence_basis: The successful event directly records creation of a durable authentication credential.

mappings.title: Account Manipulation: Additional Cloud Credentials
mappings.rationale: The event creates an additional credential that can preserve cloud account access.

rules.title: Role trust policy was changed
rules.summary: Detects a successful UpdateAssumeRolePolicy event.
rules.confidence_basis: The successful event directly records a role trust-policy modification.

mappings.title: Account Manipulation: Additional Cloud Roles
mappings.rationale: Changing a role trust relationship can grant a new principal access through the cloud role.

rules.title: Audit or threat-detection control was disabled
rules.summary: Detects successful events that stop, delete, or disable CloudTrail, Config, VPC Flow Logs, Security Hub, or GuardDuty controls.
rules.confidence_basis: The successful event directly records a monitoring or detection control being weakened; severity rises for the most disruptive actions.

mappings.title: Disable or Modify Tools
mappings.rationale: The detector directly observes successful actions that disable cloud logging, monitoring, or threat-detection tooling.

mappings.title: Disable or Modify Tools: Disable or Modify Cloud Log
mappings.rationale: The sub-technique precisely describes the logging events covered by this broader rule, but not every supported threat-detection event.

rules.title: KMS key was disabled or scheduled for deletion
rules.summary: Detects a successful DisableKey or ScheduleKeyDeletion event for an AWS KMS key.
rules.confidence_basis: The successful event directly records KMS key disruption; scheduled deletion raises severity because it can make data unrecoverable.

mappings.title: Data Destruction
mappings.rationale: Disabling or deleting a KMS key can deny access to dependent encrypted data, but impact depends on timing, recovery, and key dependencies.
